food_name: Miso Soup (Prepared, White Shiro Miso, with Tofu and Wakame)
food_subgroup: Japanese fermented miso paste-based clear soup with dashi broth, silken tofu, and wakame seaweed; prepared dish

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for miso soup as a food product in available reference; left null.

allergens: gluten (miso paste may contain barley or wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of prepared miso soup (approximately 250ml liquid volume, weighing ~280g including ~230ml dashi broth, ~40g silken tofu, and ~2g dried wakame seaweed rehydrated), as served at Japanese restaurants and izakayas in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Miso soup, prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16119 miso + component calculation for prepared soup with tofu and wakame)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for miso paste (NDB 16119) and silken tofu (NDB 16126), combined with USDA data for wakame seaweed (NDB 11667) and dashi broth, weighted by recipe proportions: ~18g white miso paste + ~230ml dashi broth + ~40g silken tofu + ~2g wakame per 280g serving, then expressed per 100g. Values cross-checked against input record which cited MEXT 8th Edition; MEXT is not an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, so USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved equivalent. Values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy component data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soy-based and fermented foods — analogous estimate based on miso and tofu GI data from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for prepared miso soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~20 (low) based on: (1) soy-based foods including tofu and miso paste have GI values of 15–25 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) the soup is predominantly water-based broth with negligible digestible starch; (3) fermented soy protein and tofu protein slow gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 280g serving calculated as: (GI 20 × 7g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 1.4 (negligible). This estimation is consistent with published literature on fermented soy foods.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 778mg sodium per 280g bowl (278mg per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: One standard bowl of miso soup provides approximately 778mg sodium, representing ~39% of the WHO 2,000mg daily sodium limit; hypertensive patients should limit miso soup to one bowl per day and avoid additional high-sodium foods at the same meal, or request reduced-miso preparation at restaurants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (~1.4 per bowl) and soy isoflavone content with mild insulin-sensitising properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Miso soup has a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per bowl (GI ~20, ~7g available carbohydrate per serving), making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; soy isoflavones in miso paste (genistein) may provide mild insulin-sensitising benefit with regular consumption, though the primary sodium concern requires monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium per serving offset by low saturated fat, soy isoflavones, and omega-3 fatty acids from dashi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While miso soup is low in saturated fat (0.28g per bowl) and contains cardioprotective soy isoflavones and dashi-derived EPA/DHA, the high sodium content (~778mg per bowl) is a significant concern for cardiovascular disease patients; consumption should be limited to one bowl per day within a sodium-controlled dietary pattern, consistent with American Heart Association guidance on sodium restriction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones with mild anti-inflammatory properties; iodine from dashi and wakame supporting thyroid function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Miso soup provides soy isoflavones (~10mg per bowl) with mild anti-inflammatory and oestrogenic properties relevant for postmenopausal women with rheumatoid arthritis, and iodine (~50–70mcg per bowl from kombu dashi and wakame) supporting thyroid function, which is more frequently dysregulated in RA patients; selenium (~4.2mcg per bowl) contributes to antioxidant defence.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso soup (味噌汁, misoshiru) is a traditional Japanese clear soup prepared by dissolving fermented soybean paste (miso) in dashi broth, typically served with silken tofu cubes, rehydrated wakame seaweed, and sliced spring onion. This record represents a standard Singapore restaurant / izakaya preparation: approximately 18g white (shiro) miso paste dissolved in ~230ml dashi broth (made from kombu kelp and katsuobushi dried bonito flakes), with ~40g silken tofu and ~2g dried wakame, yielding a 280g bowl. White shiro miso is the mildest and lowest-sodium variety of miso. Per 100g: 25 kcal, 2.0g protein, 0.9g fat (0.1g saturated), 2.5g carbohydrate, 0.5g dietary fibre, 278mg sodium. Per 280g bowl: ~70 kcal, ~5.6g protein, ~2.5g fat, ~7g carbohydrate, ~778mg sodium. Glycemic index ~20 (low); glycemic load ~1.4 per bowl (negligible). Sodium is the primary clinical concern at ~778mg per bowl (~39% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit). Miso soup is a staple of Japanese cuisine consumed at every traditional meal (breakfast, lunch, dinner) and is ubiquitous at Japanese restaurants, sushi bars, ramen shops, and izakayas in Singapore. Fermented by Aspergillus oryzae (koji), miso contains soy isoflavones (~10mg per bowl), beneficial for mild anti-inflammatory effects. Dashi provides iodine (~50–70mcg per bowl from kombu) and trace EPA/DHA from katsuobushi. Suitable for Type 2 Diabetes (negligible GL). Moderate-high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium. Halal-certified preparations are available. Contains soy and fish allergens; may contain gluten from barley or wheat in miso paste.
common_user_queries: miso soup for high blood pressure
how many calories in a bowl of miso soup
meal_context: side soup with sushi or sashimi

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for miso paste, silken tofu, and wakame seaweed — all approved primary sources — combined by recipe proportion calculation. Values are consistent with the input record and with published literature on prepared miso soup. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous soy-food data with clear documentation. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and verified. High confidence is warranted given strong approved-source grounding.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (monounsaturated fraction) for prepared miso soup as a composite dish is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the prepared soup form; the individual miso paste (NDB 16119) and silken tofu (NDB 16126) SR Legacy entries do not provide sufficient monounsaturated fatty acid detail to calculate a reliable composite per-100g value. Source consulted: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2026-04-16. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: polyunsaturated fatty acid breakdown for this prepared composite dish is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the prepared soup form; individual component entries lack sufficient polyunsaturated fatty acid granularity for reliable composite calculation. Source consulted: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the prepared composite dish (miso paste dissolved in dashi broth with tofu and wakame) is not directly reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation; while the soup is predominantly water by weight, a precise per-100g water figure for the composite is not available from the approved source. Source consulted: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for miso paste and tofu per USDA SR Legacy. cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based miso and tofu components; dashi contributes negligible cholesterol at preparation dilution. vitamin_c_mg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this preparation per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 — miso and tofu contain no significant B12; katsuobushi dashi contributes negligible B12 at dilution. vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this preparation. Input record cited MEXT 8th Edition as primary source; MEXT is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Nutrient values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved equivalent and are consistent. Halal status: standard dashi uses katsuobushi (bonito/fish), which is halal; however, some miso pastes may contain non-halal additives — halal certification should be verified per specific product. GI source is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database soy-food data; no direct GI measurement for prepared miso soup was found in the database as of 2026-04-16. Hindi local name (जापानी मिसो सूप) is a transliteration/descriptive form as no established Hindi culinary term exists for miso soup.
